feedback.text: The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.

sections.title: 3) Credit Card companies as moral guardians
path: 3) Credit Card companies as moral guardians

paragraphs.text: Greetings in all The Digital Fairness Act sounds good but I noticed few things that might be worth to look into:
Artificial lifespan of digital products.

Aka. "Stop Killing games". Games are practically functionally products with their distinct life span and fans, but companies try to sell the idea that are licenses. Gaming companies can shut down support of game on will, example Nintendo claims a right to monitor it and destroy it if they disagree with how you use it, forcing users to destroy their own games.

This restrictive phenomena is unfair towards the user and serves only to forcefull sell their next product. However game 2, might not be game 1, and fans might like game 1 more. This kind of forced retiring date is additionally making historical preservation of games impossible. https://www.stopkillinggames.com/ has lists of games delisted against customer wishes.

As a game developer I can attest that most games do have single player or local server during development phase, and even publishing source code after support has ended could enable fans to create their personally hosted services. There are many examples where old games get fan support after tens of years.
Examples: Wildstar MMORPG 2011-2018 by NCSoft, which has several fan projects to recreate whole server structure from scratch. Or SWAT 4 2005-2014 which is still recieving mod support to this day.

With older games continued support is possible, but newer games subscribed to "game as service" thinking, in my opinion are unfair digital practices which could be solved easily. As a game developer I attest that even multiplayer games have local multiplayer during development base. Games industry claims that continued support wouldn't be economically feasible is plain wrong and lazy. Even if the game multiplayer code would use licensed modules, server structure could be published 'as it is' with anyone wanting to host their service needing to agree on another license. OR game would have local single player after support is ended (which most games do have during their development).
Childrens rights are not always respected by age ratings.
In summary this is collection of serveral issues.
PEGI monopoly on european age ratings and lacks in the system.
Misclassification of some content to higher than needed age ratings.
There are no sure way to change wrongly issued age ratings.

PEGI monopoly of age ratings ment that one system dictated age ratings. In Finland this saw age rating 15 to be bumped to 16 and age ratings taken 'as they are'. However when I was in dialog both with finnish officials of MEKU and subsequently PEGI, I noticed there is no way to change age ratings percieved wrong. PEGI in their own words aimed to be harsh age rating system, not taking account of distancing effects or uncanny valley, hence not actually giving age rating based on real psychological impact like national age ratings
for example finland would give. Today similar approach is taken by Nintendo Switch games that are often given harsh age ratings as PEGI criteria can be interpreted that way. For example visual

novel murder mystery Overboard! got 18 just because in one scene blackjack is played - I would prefer game evaluated as whole and if the whole game is not about gambling 16 would suffice as loot box and similiar mechanics are not penalized in other lower age rating games. Today similiar approach is taken by Nintendo Switch games that are often given harsh age ratings.

PEGI classification treats zombies as humans, while people would rather see them as monsters because uncanny valley, hence 16 would be more appropriate than 18. Additionally 18 is criminal age rating in many european countries. Product that would not get classified by Finnish or even UK own age rating systems got repeatedly 18 markings, which means that fines and sanctions could be higher than lower age ratings. Further expatriated by the fact that some european countries have 16 as top enforceable age rating, however for those countries that enforce all ratings 18 shouldn't be given as 'suggestion'. PEGI hasn't really much changed their age ratings save for few, while Finnish audiovisual programme classifiers reclassify some 20-40 videos and films every year, both lowering and raising them. However PEGI SA can always hide under fact that its 'independent company' 'enforced by EU' or 'it would be too expensive to change age rating'.

Talking to them is like talking to stone, hence any under 18 person doesn't have de facto rights or ways to influence age ratings given. Even if classifying age rating 16 or even 12 to 18, would be breach of their rights. In digital age artificial enforcing of too high age ratings only diminishes peoples want to follow them, teaches to children that they do not matter, and makes impossible to make difference between harming 18 rated material and games classified there by technicality. Furthermore it would be useful for EU nations to have easier and cheaper ways for citizens to classify content on internet and remove monopoly on content review. Most of games are published without any age rating, which makes educators work difficult.

section_path: 3) Credit Card companies as moral guardians
paragraphs.text: As you have probably heard some 400+ games from Steam and 20000 titles from Itch.io were removed because Credit Companies view adult content as 'risk' and have bullied store contents to remove them. While this was started by Australian Activist group, this is not singular instance. Japanese company had to stop producing visual novels because credit companies viewed their content as risk. Now while this is a cultural issue, for sexuality and nudity japan and US couldn't be too far, it should be storefronts legal burden to provide ways that underage people can't buy adult stuff, it shouldn't credit companies task to be moral guardians. The credit company shouldn't dictate what adult can and cannot buy if it is legal. Stores like steam do and can adjust to individual countries legislation and even age ratings. However, they are not currently mandated to have age ratings by EU or US. Again, it is up to legislation to direct them, not credit company. Credit companies are about as important as infrastructure, water or electricity, they shouldn't make moral judgements. While freedom of speech shouldn't be overused, there is short step from censorship to censoring arts, politics and other dialog. Itch.io was place where any game developer can publish their game, banning game only because tag says sexual themes or violence, is censorship. Dialog on touchy topics like religion or sexual violence needs to happen in art, but delisting games just because vocal minority doesn't like them is plain wrong.

section_path: 3) Credit Card companies as moral guardians
paragraphs.text: Rami Sihvo, teacher, game developer and audiovisual content classifier.
